cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this question. The story is about an employer calculating how much money Julia, the governess, should get. He starts by saying they agreed on 30 rubles a month, but Julia says 40. He insists on 30 and then subtracts various days when she didn't work, like Sundays, holidays, days when the kids were sick, days she had a toothache, etc. Then he adds more deductions for breaking things and the kids getting into trouble. He keeps taking money away, and Julia just stays silent, even though he's clearly giving her less than she's owed. At the end, he gets angry when she says "merci" and realizes she's been treated poorly elsewhere. Then he gives her the full 80 rubles instead. The question is why the employer made all those criticisms. The choices are: A) She had neglected her duties. B) He wanted to pay her as little money as possible. C) He wanted to make her realize she was being cheated and protest. D) He wanted to make her feel miserable. So first, I need to see if the criticisms were genuine or not. The employer keeps subtracting things, but some of them seem like excuses to pay her less. For example, he mentions subtracting for Sundays when she only took walks, but maybe that's part of her job? Also, the deductions for the teacup, the torn jacket, stolen shoes—maybe those were not her fault. The key
